articleTitle: ReCoSplat: Autoregressive Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting Using Render-and-Compare
explanation:
Just like Naruto struggled with the fundamental paradox of controlling the Nine-Tails' power - needing to trust the fox's chakra while simultaneously maintaining his own stability - ReCoSplat tackles a critical distribution mismatch problem in online novel view synthesis. When training with ground-truth camera poses, the model achieves stability during learning, but this creates a dangerous gap when it must rely on predicted poses at inference time. This is the classic "training-inference mismatch" that plagues autoregressive systems: like Naruto practicing with perfect chakra control in training but facing chaotic battle conditions, the model's performance degrades when real-world imperfect pose estimates replace the pristine ground truth data it learned on.

The technical brilliance of ReCoSplat lies in its Render-and-Compare (ReCo) module, which acts like Naruto's development of Sage Mode - providing a constant feedback mechanism to maintain balance even when conditions become unstable. The ReCo module renders the current Gaussian Splatting reconstruction from the predicted camera viewpoint and directly compares it with the incoming observation frame, generating a stable conditioning signal that compensates for pose estimation errors. This autoregressive feed-forward architecture assembles local 3D Gaussians in camera space rather than canonical space, achieving better scalability much like how Naruto learned to distribute his chakra more efficiently across shadow clones. The system supports both posed and unposed inputs with or without camera intrinsics, making it as versatile as Naruto's ability to adapt his fighting style to any opponent.

To handle long sequences without overwhelming computational resources - the equivalent of Naruto learning to manage hundreds of shadow clones without chakra exhaustion - ReCoSplat introduces a hybrid KV cache compression strategy that's genuinely innovative. The approach combines early-layer truncation with chunk-level selective retention, achieving over 90% reduction in KV cache size for sequences exceeding 100 frames while maintaining performance. This is like Naruto's mastery of the Multiple Shadow Clone Technique: instead of maintaining every clone indefinitely (which would drain his chakra), he strategically dismisses early clones while retaining the most informative ones. The technical implementation involves truncating attention key-value pairs from earlier transformer layers while selectively retaining chunks of information that provide maximum utility for ongoing scene reconstruction.

The performance results demonstrate state-of-the-art achievement across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution benchmarks, with the system maintaining robust novel view synthesis quality even under challenging unposed input conditions. Like Naruto eventually surpassing all previous Hokage through his unique combination of techniques, ReCoSplat's fusion of autoregressive Gaussian Splatting with render-and-compare feedback creates a new paradigm for online 3D scene reconstruction. The model's ability to handle sequential, often unposed observations while maintaining computational efficiency represents a significant leap toward practical real-time novel view synthesis applications. Just as Naruto proved that the most powerful techniques come not from raw strength but from understanding how to balance different forces, ReCoSplat shows that the future of 3D scene understanding lies in intelligently combining pose estimation, scene representation, and adaptive feedback mechanisms.
